findings.message: `signal_check_gate` treats the resolved strictness as if it were an override when calling the per-chain helpers, but `strict_override=false` means "load config and resolve again" in those helpers. For any interim cell, this re-reads config from CWD with `gate=None` and drops the `workspace_root`/selected gate already used by the aggregate command, so `signal check --gate ... --workspace-root ...` can fail or evaluate against the wrong matrix instead of the one loaded at line 459. The aggregate path needs to dispatch with the already-resolved boolean directly, or use a helper that accepts effective strictness without re-resolving.

`catalogue_hash` is no longer validated before running the chain checks. This change deleted the `parse_catalogue_hash` helper and now passes user input directly to the infrastructure checks, which only compare the raw string against the stored canonical hash. That makes malformed or empty hashes flow through the gate path as if they were a current catalogue hash, producing stale/missing-file findings instead of the intended hash-parse failure, and violates the boundary requirement that external input be validated via the `ContentHash` domain type. The same pattern is repeated in the aggregate check for chains 2 and 3, so restore validation before dispatching to these checks.

`signal_calc_adr_user` still delegates to `AdrUserChain::calc_live`, but that usecase method is the not-yet-wired placeholder and always returns an error. As a result, the new public `sotp signal calc-adr-user` command fails for every invocation instead of performing the live ADR scan that the signal namespace is meant to expose. Wire this path through the same infrastructure-backed ADR scanner used by `signal_check_adr_user` or another real calc helper.

`signal_calc_spec_adr` delegates to `SpecAdrChain::calc`, which is also still a placeholder returning `SpecAdrCalcError("calc not yet wired")`. This leaves `sotp signal calc-spec-adr` permanently unable to refresh `spec.json` signals, so the command family cannot recover from the stale-signal failures produced by `signal check-spec-adr`. The composition root should call the existing spec read/evaluate/write path rather than the placeholder usecase method.

`signal_check_gate` still passes the already-resolved strictness booleans into the public per-chain helpers. When `catalog_strict` or `impl_strict` is `false`, those helpers treat `false` as “no --strict override”, call `resolve_strict` again with `gate=None`, and default back to the commit gate using a fresh config read. This makes merge-gate lenient/strict decisions for chains 2 and 3 incorrect whenever they differ from commit, and also defeats the intended single matrix load. Dispatch directly to the underlying chain checks with the resolved booleans instead of re-entering `resolve_strict`.

`workspace_root` is applied to config loading and signal-file path construction, but the layer reader is still created with `LocalSignalLayerReaderAdapter::discover()`, which resolves from the process CWD. For `signal check-catalog-spec`, `signal check-impl-catalog`, and aggregate chain ②/③ with `--workspace-root`, this can read the active track, enabled layers, and catalogue bytes from a different repository while checking files under the requested root. Construct the reader from the resolved `root` instead so all inputs come from the same workspace.

`signal_check_layer_chain_with_strict` builds every signals path as `<layer_id>{suffix}`. That is valid for catalogue-spec signals, but `signal check-impl-catalog` passes `-type-signals.json` here while type-signals are written via `TdddLayerBinding::signal_file()`, which is derived from `catalogue_file` (for example, `custom-types.json` writes `custom-type-signals.json`, not `domain-type-signals.json`). Repositories using custom catalogue file names will therefore fail `signal check-impl-catalog` and aggregate `signal check` even after `signal calc-impl-catalog` regenerated the correct file. Resolve the impl-catalog signal filename from the layer binding instead of suffixing the layer id.

`signal_check_catalog_spec` now runs the generic layer checker over every `tddd.enabled` layer and derives a catalogue-spec signals path for each one, but it never honors `TdddLayerBinding::catalogue_spec_signal_enabled()`. Existing catalogue-spec gate behavior skips layers that have not opted into `tddd.catalogue_spec_signal.enabled`; with this path, an enabled TDDD layer that is intentionally opted out will be checked anyway and will fail on a missing `<layer>-catalogue-spec-signals.json` file. The aggregate `signal check --gate` uses the same helper, so the gate can fail for inactive Chain 2 layers. Filter disabled catalogue-spec bindings or make the Chain 2 reader/helper enumerate only opted-in layers.

The opt-out check for catalogue-spec layers happens inside the per-layer closure, but `usecase::signal::check_catalog_spec` invokes that closure only after `LocalSignalLayerReaderAdapter::catalogue_bytes` has already read and symlink-guarded the layer's catalogue. As a result, `signal check-catalog-spec` and aggregate chain ② can still fail on a layer with `catalogue_spec_signal.enabled = false` if that layer has an unreadable or symlinked catalogue, even though existing catalogue-spec paths skip opt-out bindings before touching layer files. Filter chain ② to opted-in bindings before catalogue reads, or use a catalogue-spec-specific reader.

`signal_calc_catalog_spec` has the same late opt-out filter: `usecase::signal::calc_catalog_spec` reads each TDDD layer's catalogue before this closure can return `pass()` for `catalogue_spec_signal.enabled = false`. This makes `signal calc-catalog-spec` fail on opted-out layers with unreadable/symlinked catalogue files instead of skipping them, unlike the existing track catalogue-spec refresh path.

`AssocTypeDecl.name` and `AssocConstDecl.name` are plain `String`s, unlike the rest of `catalogue_v2`'s identifier-bearing fields which use validated newtypes. That lets callers construct invalid associated-item names inside the domain model and defers validation to later layers, which violates the repo's "illegal states unrepresentable" rule for a new public API surface.

`AssocConstName` is a new public validation boundary, but this module adds no direct tests for valid construction, invalid-input rejection, or `Display`/`FromStr` round-trips. Because its validation logic is macro-backed and shared only indirectly with the other identifier wrappers, a regression here would not be caught.

`TraitEntry` now carries `assoc_types` and `assoc_consts`, but there is no test coverage asserting their default-empty construction or that they participate in `PartialEq` the way the existing `generics` and `where_predicates` fields do. That leaves the new trait-item shape under-tested and makes a wiring regression easy to miss.

`test_trait_entry_with_non_empty_informal_grounds_stores_grounding` still constructs `TraitEntry` inline with all fields instead of using `trait_entry_fixture()` and mutating `informal_grounds`. This leaves a TraitEntry-related test outside the intended DRY refactor and will keep this test coupled to future field additions.

The briefing says this domain scope is a test-only DRY refactor in `libs/domain/src/tddd/catalogue_v2/entries.rs`, with no production code or public API changes. This diff instead adds a new production dependency plus a public `hash_util` module/function, so the branch no longer matches the stated design intent or strict modification boundary for this scope.

`sha256_hex` is exposed as a public domain API but has no domain-level test covering its output contract, despite the review policy requiring happy-path coverage for public APIs. Add a focused test, for example checking the known SHA-256 hex for empty input or a fixed byte string.

Makefile.toml is explicitly outside the harness-policy/T004 modification boundary: the briefing says Makefile migrations belong to T011 and that this config is read by tests only until T006/T011. This diff wires signal-gate config into CI/commit tasks now, giving the config production effects in the config-only task.

The new chain ③ task skips a layer whenever `<layer>-type-signals.json` is absent, even if the corresponding `<layer>-types.json` catalogue exists. That bypasses `check-impl-catalog`'s fail-closed missing-signal behavior, so `cargo make ci` can pass after a generated type-signals file is deleted instead of blocking until it is regenerated.

The chain ② task similarly skips missing `<layer>-catalogue-spec-signals.json` files before invoking `signal check-catalog-spec`. With `catalog_spec` strict at the commit gate, a layer with a catalogue but no persisted catalog-spec signals can pass CI instead of failing closed on the missing signal file.

The pre-review gate now tells review-fix-lead agents to run `bin/sotp signal calc-impl-catalog` and `bin/sotp signal calc-catalog-spec` without arguments. Both new subcommands require `--signals-path` and `--catalog-hash`, so this sequence exits with clap usage errors before `bin/sotp review local` can record a round. The prompt template in `.claude/commands/track/review.md` repeats the same invalid commands, so generated reviewer prompts will inherit the failure. Document the existing per-layer `track-active-gate` wrapper or include the full per-layer argument loop.

The native-command example `bin/sotp signal calc-spec-adr` is incomplete: the replacement command requires `--spec-json <SPEC_JSON>`, and running it as documented exits with a missing-argument error. This leaves the dev-environment command reference with an unusable replacement for the removed `bin/sotp track signals` workflow.

The new pre-review command cannot be invoked as written: `Makefile.toml` marks `track-active-gate` as a private cargo-make task, and `cargo make track-active-gate` exits with `Task track-active-gate is private` unless `--allow-private` is passed. Because this file and `.claude/commands/track/review.md:105` now tell review-fix agents to run that exact command before every reviewer invocation, the Claude review loop fails before recording any canonical review round. Use `cargo make --allow-private track-active-gate`, expose a public wrapper, or keep the native command sequence.

The pre-review workflow now tells the review-fix agent to run both signal calculators for every TDDD-active layer and compute `--catalog-hash` from the corresponding `<layer>-types.json`. In this repo all three layers are TDDD-enabled, but many tracks legitimately do not have all three catalogue files yet; the shared `track-active-gate` skips absent catalogues before hashing them. Following this new policy literally will fail before `bin/sotp review local` on Phase <2 or single-layer tracks. Carry over the absent-catalogue skip behavior here, or point agents at the shared gate that already implements it.

Top-level JSON shape is not validated before calling `.get("$schema_version")`, so a syntactically valid but non-object document (for example `[]` or `"foo"`) is reported as `MissingKey { key: "$schema_version" }` instead of the documented parse/structure failure. That misclassifies a malformed config and makes the error contract inconsistent.

Gate-cell type errors fall through to `SignalGatesConfigError::ParseFailed { path: PathBuf::new(), ... }` because `find_invalid_strictness_cell` only checks string values and ignores numbers/nulls/arrays/objects. A config like `"adr_user": 1` therefore loses the real file path and offending key/value, violating the loader's error-reporting contract.

`read_adr_verify_report` now scans `repo_root/knowledge/adr` directly, but it no longer applies the trusted-root / symlink rejection that `execute_verify_adr_signals` uses. A branch can make `knowledge/adr` a symlink and redirect the merge-gate scan outside the repository, which is a path-traversal/security regression.

The new qualified-path branch accepts any `qself` path but only reads the single segment at `qself.position`. If the path has additional trailing segments after the associated item, they are silently dropped instead of remaining unresolved, so valid inputs can be misrepresented as a different type.

The new QualifiedPath tests only assert a few fields (`name`, presence of `trait_`, `args`) and never compare the full `Type::QualifiedPath` literal. That leaves the main D1 regression risk untested: the builder could still emit the wrong `self_type` or other nested shape and these tests would pass. The briefing explicitly called for full `assert_eq!` coverage here.

`read_adr_verify_report` is a new public merge-gate adapter entry point, but the added coverage only checks the symlink rejection path. There is no test for the expected success path or for a non-symlink I/O/parse failure, so a break in the new ADR scan wiring or error mapping would ship without regression coverage.

`Ok(false)` from `reject_symlinks_below` is not equivalent to a cleanly missing `knowledge/adr` directory. If `knowledge/` is replaced with a regular file or another non-directory ancestor, this branch returns `NotFound` and the merge gate will treat ADR scanning as absent, bypassing chain 0 instead of failing closed.

`read_adr_verify_report` delegates to `FsAdrFileAdapter`, which skips non-regular entries instead of rejecting them. That means symlinked `knowledge/adr/*.md` files are silently ignored, so a branch can hide all ADR decisions behind symlinks and get an empty clean report instead of a fail-closed error.

The new `TraitImplDeclV2.for_type` generic-param path is only covered by parser unit tests. There is no end-to-end codec regression test that exercises `encode(doc)` with `impl_generics = ["T"]` and `for_type = "T"`, so regressions in the new callsite plumbing or `normalize_impl_for_type_path` would not be caught.

`AssocTypeDecl.default` and `AssocConstDecl.ty` are only checked with `TypeRef::new`, which accepts any non-empty string. Unlike the other `TypeRef` fields in this codec, these values are never run through `validate_type_ref_str`, so malformed Rust types (for example `Vec<` or `Foo<`) now survive decode and fail later in the encode path instead of being rejected at the DTO boundary.

`AssocTypeDecl` defaults are parsed with `parse_type_ref_str` but never rewritten against `trait_generic_names`. That means defaults such as `type Item = Vec<T>` are still rendered with a raw `T` path instead of `Type::Generic("T")`, so traits that only differ by generic parameter renames will compare unequal even though the bounds path already canonicalizes those names.

The new assoc-item decoders only reject empty names. `assoc_types[*].name` and `assoc_consts[*].name` are never validated as Rust identifiers, and there is no duplicate-name check before the items are fed into `Trait.items` / `build_trait_method_map`. A malformed or colliding assoc item can therefore be accepted and silently overwrite a different item during trait fingerprinting, producing a false match.

`assoc_const_decl_from_dto` accepts `default_value: Some("")` with no validation. That collapses to the same fingerprint as `None` later (`unwrap_or("")` in trait comparison), so malformed wire input can silently masquerade as a missing associated-const value and change trait equality results.

`encode_assoc_type_item()` and `encode_assoc_const_item()` still call `parse_type_ref_str()` without any trait-generic context, then rely on `rewrite_generic_types()`. That rewrite does not descend into `Type::QualifiedPath`, so valid defaults/types like `T::Item`, `<T as Trait>::Assoc`, or `Vec<T::Assoc>` will keep a non-generic self-type and can be emitted as the wrong rustdoc shape. The new assoc-item codec will therefore mis-encode valid trait-generic inputs instead of matching the C-side representation.

This appends `AssocType`/`AssocConst` IDs into `Trait.items`, but the contract-map renderer still walks `Trait.items` and emits only `ItemEnum::Function` children. As a result, traits with associated items now influence equality/signals but those items remain invisible in rendered output, so the D4 change is internally inconsistent.

`validate_trait_item_names` now enforces a single flat name set across methods, associated types, and associated consts. That rejects valid traits that reuse an identifier across namespaces (for example, an associated type and a method with the same name), so the codec will fail on some legal catalogues instead of round-tripping them.

`validate_trait_item_names` now accepts legal cross-namespace name reuse (`type Item; fn Item();` and `type Item; const Item: ...;`), but the trait comparer still flattens every trait item into a single `BTreeMap<String, String>` keyed only by bare `name`. Once assoc types/consts are emitted into `Trait.items`, those legal traits become order-dependent or collide during comparison, so the same source can compare unequal depending on declaration order.

P1: `resolve_external_type_ids` still only recurses through `ResolvedPath` and container variants, but the new assoc-item encoder now emits `Type::QualifiedPath` values. Any external crate ids inside `self_type` or `trait_` stay unresolved, so valid trait-scoped defaults like `<ext::Foo as ext::Trait>::Assoc` can compare/resolve incorrectly. See [encoder_state_type_ref_parsing.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core-002/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/catalogue_to_extended_crate_codec/encoder_state_type_ref_parsing.rs#L32) and [encoder_state_fn_trait_codec.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core-002/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/catalogue_to_extended_crate_codec/encoder_state_fn_trait_codec.rs#L559).

P1: `rewrite_trait_scoped_type_inner` does not recurse into `ImplTrait`, `DynTrait`, `FunctionPointer`, or `Pat`, so trait projections nested inside those forms are left as plain `ResolvedPath` nodes. Inputs like `Vec<fn() -> T::Item>` or `impl Iterator<Item = T::Item>` will be mis-encoded instead of producing the expected qualified projection shape. See [encoder_state_fn_trait_codec.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core-002/libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/catalogue_to_extended_crate_codec/encoder_state_fn_trait_codec.rs#L564).

Associated type bounds are still encoded with `encode_and_validate_bound`, which only rewrites bare generic arguments. If a new `assoc_types` entry uses a valid projection in its bound, such as `type Output: Iterator<Item = T::Item>` or `<T as Trait>::Item`, the A-side bound keeps that projection as an unresolved/resolved path instead of the `Type::QualifiedPath` shape rustdoc emits. `build_trait_method_map` then fingerprints A as `#0::Item`/unresolved while C formats `<#0 as _>::Item`, so valid assoc-bearing traits can stay non-blue. The assoc type default path already uses `encode_trait_scoped_type_ref`; bounds need the same trait-scoped projection rewrite and coverage.

`encode_trait_scoped_bound` parses associated-type bounds without the enclosing trait generic context, then tries to rewrite the parsed tree afterward. Because `encode_bound_str`/`parse_generic_bound` resolves single-segment names against locals/std prelude before this rewrite runs, an in-scope generic that shadows a prelude/local name inside a bound argument is already lost. For example, `trait Port<From> { type Out: Iterator<Item = From>; }` should encode `From` as `Type::Generic("From")` to match rustdoc, but this path will first resolve it as the prelude `std::convert::From`, and `rewrite_trait_scoped_bound` cannot recover it. That makes otherwise valid associated-type bounds produce false signal mismatches. Parse bounds with the trait generic names in scope, or otherwise prevent in-scope generic names from being resolved before rewriting.

P1: `encode_trait_scoped_type_ref` now parses nested trait-generic projections like `Vec<T::Item>` through `parse_type_ref_str_with_generics`, but that parser only recognizes bare generic names. During its discovery pass it treats the `T::Item` prefix as an external crate named `T`; the later rewrite converts the type shape to `QualifiedPath` but leaves the fake external crate registered globally in the emitted `Crate`. That can pollute `external_crates` and make subsequent `T::...` references look resolved instead of unresolved. Teach the parser to handle generic-prefix multi-segment paths before external-crate registration, or pre-rewrite these projections without registering the prefix.

P1: This production module is now 748 lines with no test block exemption, exceeding the repository's 700-line maximum for production modules. The new associated-item encoding and trait-scoped rewrite helpers should be split into a sibling module, similar to the `decode.rs` split done for the same size rule.

`try_build_generic_projection` bypasses the normal parser for `T::Assoc`-style associated projections but only validates the first character of the associated item name. Since `TypeRef::new` only rejects empty strings, an in-memory catalogue can now encode invalid Rust type refs such as `T::Item-foo` as `Type::QualifiedPath` instead of returning `InvalidTypeRef`, unlike the other encode paths. Validate the full identifier or fall through to the parser before constructing the qualified path.

`parse_type_ref_str_with_generics` forwards to the normal parser without shadowing local catalogue names. That parser still receives `local_name_to_id`, so a legal generic impl such as `impl<T> Trait for T` is encoded as the local catalogue type `T` whenever the catalogue also declares a type named `T`, instead of `Type::Generic("T")`. Other trait-scoped paths avoid this by temporarily shadowing local names before parsing; this wrapper needs the same precedence or the A-side impl target can mismatch rustdoc for generic impls under a local-name collision.

The new `assoc_types` and `assoc_consts` catalogue wire-format fields are not exercised by the `CatalogueDocumentCodec` tests: there is no decode, encode/round-trip, or invalid-input test covering these DTO fields. Since this is a public JSON schema extension, regressions in serde defaults, newtype validation, duplicate-name rejection, or `default_value` mapping could pass CI. Add focused happy-path and error-case codec tests for associated types and associated consts.

`verify_sibling_json_stages()` returns immediately on any Stage 1 error (`return Some(stage1)`), so the combined `verify()` path never reports Stage 2 findings when `spec.json` is stale or otherwise invalid. This contradicts the briefing requirement that the combined caller merge Stage 1 and Stage 2 outcomes, and it can silently hide type-signal violations behind an earlier spec-adr failure.

P1: `verify_sibling_json_stages` always invokes and merges both stage functions, so a symlink-guard failure on `spec.json` is reported twice (once from each stage). That violates the briefing’s no-double-counting requirement and inflates `VerifyOutcome::error_count()` for a single underlying failure. Short-circuit stage 2 when stage 1 fails on the shared file/symlink preamble, or dedupe identical findings before merging.

`verify_type_signals_from_spec_json` now accepts a `spec_json_path`, but after the symlink/existence guard it never reads or decodes that spec file before evaluating the type-signal catalogues. That means the new standalone Stage 2 entrypoint can return success for a track whose `spec.json` is malformed or unreadable as long as the catalogue signal files are valid, whereas the original combined path failed closed on spec decode errors and the briefing calls for the same shared read/decode preamble in both stage functions. Add the same spec.json read/decode check here before `load_tddd_layers` so `check-impl-catalog` cannot pass with an invalid spec document.

`check_catalogue_spec_signals` only evaluates Red/Yellow signals, but this caller now delegates to it without first validating signal freshness. The path reads both the current catalogue and the persisted signals document, yet only checks count and positional names before merging the gate result; it never compares `doc.catalogue_declaration_hash` or per-entry `entry_hash` values against the current catalogue. A stale all-Blue signals file with the same entries can therefore pass strict verification after catalogue grounding changes. Add the freshness check before this delegation.

P1 testing gap: `test_v3_catalogue_signals_referencing_nonexistent_type_produces_finding` does not exercise the missing-entry path it claims to cover. It writes `V3_CATALOGUE_EMPTY` plus all-zero hashes, so `execute_catalogue_spec_signals` exits earlier on the coverage-mismatch branch before the new freshness / domain-gate delegation is reached. A regression in the real non-existent-type path would still pass this test.

These two new `pub` helpers are only exercised indirectly by the module tests. `compute_catalogue_entry_hash` has untested failure paths (`invalid JSON`, missing section/key), and `compute_catalogue_declaration_hash` expands the public surface without a direct contract test. Either keep them `pub(crate)` if they are test-only, or add direct happy-path and error-path coverage.

`check_catalog_spec_from_signals_file` verifies only the document-level `catalogue_declaration_hash` before delegating to `check_catalogue_spec_signals`, but that domain function explicitly assumes the caller has already performed coverage, positional-name, and per-entry `entry_hash` freshness checks. Because this helper is wired as the direct `signal check-catalog-spec` path and receives only `signals_path` plus a hash, a truncated or hand-edited signals file with the current declaration hash and Blue remaining entries can pass while catalogue entries are missing or stale. Reuse the integrity checks from `execute_catalogue_spec_signals` or pass enough catalogue context to perform them here before the domain gate call.

`check_impl_catalog_from_signals_file` reads `signals_path` directly with `read_to_string`, so the explicit `check-impl-catalog` entrypoint follows symlinked signal files. The existing guarded path rejects symlinks before reading, and this function's own contract says symlinked signal files should fail closed. Add trusted-root symlink validation before the read, or route this command through the existing guarded path.

`check_catalog_spec_from_signals_file` derives `catalogue_path` and then reads it directly with `std::fs::read_to_string` without applying the shared symlink guard. The existing `execute_catalogue_spec_signals` path rejects symlinked catalogue files before reading, but the new direct `signal check-catalog-spec` path will follow a symlinked `<layer>-types.json`; because the caller also hashes that same path, crafted linked catalogue/signals content can satisfy the hash check while bypassing the fail-closed symlink policy. Guard `catalogue_path` and its ancestors before reading it.

The hand-rolled signals symlink check only stats the leaf and immediate parent. If an earlier component is a symlink, such as `track/items/<track-id>` pointing elsewhere while `<track-id>/<layer>-catalogue-spec-signals.json` itself is regular, both `symlink_metadata` calls follow that ancestor and `read_to_string` reads through it. This regresses the existing `reject_symlinks_below` behavior, which rejects every component below the trusted root for this gate.

The new `check_impl_catalog_from_signals_file` has the same partial symlink guard: it checks only `signals_path` and its immediate parent before reading. A symlinked grandparent path is followed, so the explicit `signal check-impl-catalog` entrypoint can read a signal file through a symlinked track/items component even though the existing `evaluate_layer_catalogue` path rejects that via `reject_symlinks_below`.

`catalogue_path_from_signals` hard-codes `<layer>-types.json` from the catalogue-spec signals filename. TDDD already supports `tddd.catalogue_file` overrides, and the calc path resolves the real `TdddLayerBinding` before generating `<layer>-catalogue-spec-signals.json` from the custom catalogue. For such a layer, this check reads `domain-types.json` instead of the configured catalogue, causing a fresh generated signal file to fail or be checked against the wrong catalogue. The explicit check path needs the resolved catalogue path/binding rather than deriving the default name.

`check_catalog_spec_from_signals_file` treats `current_dir()` as the trusted workspace root but only calls `reject_symlinks_below`, which does not enforce that `signals_path` is actually below that root. An absolute or `..` path outside the repo with the expected `*-catalogue-spec-signals.json` basename is accepted, then the catalogue is resolved from that outside directory and can pass with a matching supplied hash. The gate should fail closed unless the resolved signals and catalogue paths are contained in the workspace/track directory, matching the existing guarded `execute_catalogue_spec_signals` path.

`check_impl_catalog_from_signals_file` has the same containment gap: `reject_symlinks_below(signals_path, &workspace_root)` rejects symlinks but still allows reading a signal file outside the workspace when given an absolute or traversing path. Since this direct `signal check-impl-catalog` path trusts the caller-provided catalogue hash, an out-of-workspace forged signal document can pass without validating the real track file. Canonicalize and require `signals_path` to stay under the workspace/track root before reading.

The containment check can be bypassed with `..` components: `Path::components().collect()` does not collapse parent-directory components, so a path like `<repo>/../outside/domain-catalogue-spec-signals.json` still starts with `workspace_root` lexically. `reject_symlinks_below` and the later reads then operate on the outside path, and the derived catalogue path has the same issue. This lets the explicit catalog-spec gate read and validate files outside the workspace despite the fail-closed containment contract.

The explicit impl-catalog gate has the same lexical containment bypass: `components().collect()` preserves `..`, and `starts_with(&workspace_root)` still accepts paths such as `<repo>/../outside/domain-type-signals.json`. The subsequent symlink guard and `read_to_string` can therefore read a signals file outside the workspace, bypassing the intended workspace-only boundary.

`check_catalog_spec_from_signals_file` derives `workspace_root` from `std::env::current_dir()`, so a valid `--signals-path` under the repo is rejected whenever the command is invoked from a repo subdirectory or with an absolute repo path while CWD is not the repo root. That makes the new chain ② gate depend on caller CWD instead of the actual workspace root; pass/discover the repo root before doing containment and architecture-rules lookups.

`check_impl_catalog_from_signals_file` has the same CWD-as-workspace-root assumption, so valid type-signal files under the repository fail the containment check when the gate is run outside the repo root. The chain ③ check should use a trusted/repo root supplied by the caller or discovered from git, not `current_dir()`.

`catalogue_spec_signals.rs` now has production code through line 753 before the `#[cfg(test)]` block, exceeding the AGENTS.md 700-line maximum for production modules. The new `check_catalog_spec_from_signals_file`/path-normalization block is large enough to push the module over the cap; split the new direct-check helper into a smaller module or reuse/extract the existing integrity-check path so this production module stays within the limit.

`check_impl_catalog_from_signals_file` pushes this production module past the AGENTS.md size limit: the `#[cfg(test)]` block now starts at line 759, so `spec_states.rs` has 758 production lines, exceeding the 700-line maximum. This scope added the 133-line direct check helper; split the new helper/path-normalization code into a smaller module or reuse an existing shared gate helper so the production module stays within the enforced bound.

`check_signals_file` is intended to support explicit `--signals-path` checks, but it discovers the workspace from the process current directory via `SystemGitRepo::discover()`. If the command is invoked from outside the target repo, or from another repo while passing an absolute signals path, valid files under the target workspace are rejected or checked against the wrong `architecture-rules.json`. Make this derive the repo from the supplied path, or pass the resolved workspace root into this helper.

This new public gate entrypoint has no direct tests. Existing tests cover `execute_catalogue_spec_signals`, but not the explicit-path path through `check_signals_file`, catalogue resolution, hash mismatch handling, or strict Yellow promotion used by `signal check-catalog-spec`; regressions in the new CLI path would not be caught.

This new public gate entrypoint has no direct tests. Existing tests cover `verify_type_signals_from_spec_json`, but not the explicit-path path through `check_signals_file`, workspace containment, hash mismatch handling, or strict Yellow promotion used by `signal check-impl-catalog`; regressions in the new CLI path would not be caught.

`execute_verify_adr_signals_with_strict` adds the strictness behavior used by `signal check-adr-user`, but the changed scope has no test exercising a Yellow ADR report with `strict=true` versus `strict=false`. Existing ADR tests only call the non-strict wrapper and cover Blue/Red/listing-error paths, so a regression that leaves Yellow as a warning in the strict gate would not be caught. Add focused tests for the new public function's Yellow handling in both strict modes.

`active_track_id` ignores the adapter's stored `workspace_root` and rediscoveres git from the process CWD. The other methods read `architecture-rules.json` and catalogue files under `self.workspace_root`, so callers using `LocalSignalLayerReaderAdapter::new(root)` or any flow where CWD differs can combine a track ID from one repository with files from another. Resolve the repo with `SystemGitRepo::discover_from(&self.workspace_root)` so all port methods operate on the same workspace.

The new public `LocalSignalLayerReaderAdapter` has no direct happy-path or error-path tests, despite doing git discovery, branch parsing, layer enumeration, and catalogue file reads. This leaves the CWD/workspace mismatch above and the documented `Ok(None)` missing-catalogue behavior unguarded. Add focused tests for `active_track_id`, `enabled_layers`, and `catalogue_bytes` using a temporary git repo and explicit workspace root.

`catalogue_bytes` hard-codes `<layer>-types.json` even though `enabled_layers` reads `architecture-rules.json` bindings that may specify a custom `tddd.catalogue_file`. For an enabled layer configured with `catalogue_file: "custom-types.json"`, this adapter will look for `track/items/<track>/<layer>-types.json`, get `NotFound`, return `Ok(None)`, and the usecase orchestrator will silently skip that layer. That can make the argless signal gates pass without checking an enabled catalogue. Re-read the layer binding here and use `binding.catalogue_file()` for the requested layer, or return an error if the layer binding is missing.

`catalogue_bytes` reads the resolved catalogue path with `std::fs::read` without applying the repository symlink guard. The layer config only validates the filename string; it does not prevent `track/items/<track>/<catalogue_file>` itself from being a symlink. In the new argless signal flow this read supplies the current catalogue hash before the explicit-path verifiers run, and `check_impl_catalog` has no later catalogue-path guard at all, so a symlinked catalogue can make the gate hash and validate content outside the workspace or leak that file's hash in stale-signal errors. This should reject symlinks below `workspace_root` before reading, matching the existing verify path-safety policy.

`test_support` is now compiled and publicly exported whenever the `test-helpers` feature is enabled, but the module contains panicking helpers (`unwrap`, `expect`, `assert!`). Because a Cargo feature is not `#[cfg(test)]`, this makes panicking code part of the feature-enabled library build, violating the repo rule that library code must not panic outside test cfg. Keep this module test-only, or expose feature-gated helpers that return `Result` instead of panicking.

When `infrastructure` is compiled as a dependency with the `test-helpers` feature, `cfg(test)` is false and this `run_git` variant returns `RunGitResult`. That wrapper is not `#[must_use]`, so dependent test setup can call `run_git(...)` and silently drop git spawn/exit failures instead of failing or propagating them. Return `Result<(), RunGitError>` directly, or mark the wrapper as `#[must_use]` and update callers to assert/propagate it.

The feature-gated `run_git` helper returns a custom `RunGitResult` that is not `#[must_use]`, so callers can ignore failures without any compiler warning. This already happens after the migration in `apps/cli-composition/src/verify.rs`, where `run_git(dir.path(), &["init"])` and the `core.hooksPath` config call now discard the result; if either git command fails, the test proceeds against an uninitialized or misconfigured directory instead of failing at setup. Return `Result<(), RunGitError>` directly or mark `RunGitResult` as `#[must_use]` and update callers to assert/propagate the result.

`#[arg(long)]` on `catalogue_hash` exposes the public flag as `--catalogue-hash`, but T009 and this module's own CLI-surface rule require the new `signal` UI to use `catalog` spelling. The same field appears in the catalog-spec and impl-catalog calc/check adapters, and the parser tests now lock in the wrong flag, so documented invocations using `--catalog-hash` will be rejected.

The aggregate `signal check` parser allows the catalog hash inputs to be omitted by defaulting both hashes to `""`, but `run_aggregate_check` always forwards those values into chain ②/③ checks, where they are parsed as SHA-256 content hashes. As a result, the advertised `sotp signal check --gate commit|merge` path fails before evaluating all four chains unless callers manually provide both hashes, so AC-04's aggregate gate is not actually runnable with the default arguments tested here.

The fallback `spec_json` path for the aggregate check is `track/items/spec.json`, but track specs in this repository live under `track/items/<track-id>/spec.json` or as `spec.json` within a track directory. With `--spec-json` omitted, the aggregate check points chain ① at a non-existent file, so the no-extra-args aggregate command cannot work from the normal repo layout.

`signal check --gate commit|merge` is specified as the aggregate command, with fallback paths when optional args are absent, but the parser now requires `--spec-json`, `--catalog-spec-hash`, and `--impl-catalog-hash` before dispatch. This means the advertised aggregate gate invocation is rejected by clap and cannot run all four chains unless callers know and provide extra arguments, breaking the aggregate gate surface described by the spec/briefing.

`CatalogSpecChain::check_freshness` only compares the document-level `catalogue_declaration_hash` with the current catalogue hash. The chain ② contract requires the persisted per-entry `CatalogueSpecSignal::entry_hash()` values to be checked against freshly computed entry hashes before `evaluate_gate`; otherwise `SoTChain::check` can accept a signals document with stale, missing, or manually trimmed per-entry signals as long as the aggregate declaration hash still matches. Carry the current entry hashes in `CatalogSpecInput` and fail closed on missing/mismatched entries before delegating to `check_catalogue_spec_signals`.

The chain-0 result is only merged at the end of the function, but the early returns here and at the later `stage1.has_errors()` check exit before that merge. Invalid branches or missing/failed `spec.json` therefore drop ADR findings entirely, which contradicts the new requirement that chain ⓪ be accumulated independently of the other stages.

The new `strict=false` arm in `check_chain2_for_layer` is not exercised by any test in this scope, and every existing test now hardcodes `all_strict_matrix()`. That leaves the new warning-mode behavior and the added ADR reader port effectively unverified, so a regression in the non-blocking path could ship unnoticed.

`check_strict_merge_gate` reads the ADR report before calling `validate_branch_ref`, so an untrusted branch string reaches the `TrackBlobReader` port unvalidated. That reintroduces the very ref-safety check this function is supposed to enforce and can make the adapter operate on malicious refs like `..` or `@{...}` before the branch is rejected.

Only `catalog_spec` is exercised with a non-strict matrix; the newly matrix-driven `adr_user`, `spec_adr`, and `impl_catalog` paths all run under `all_strict_matrix()` in tests. A swapped ChainId or accidental hardcoded strict value for any of those cells would still pass the current suite. Add focused interim-mode tests for Chain ⓪, Chain ①, and Chain ③ Yellow signals.

The new Chain ⓪ `read_adr_verify_report` error path is untested. The trait documents `FetchError` as fail-closed and this branch turns it into a blocking finding, but the added tests only cover `NotFound` and `Found` ADR reports. A regression that accidentally skipped ADR scan I/O/parse failures would still pass the current suite. Add a test with an otherwise clean gate and `BlobFetchResult::FetchError` from `read_adr_verify_report`, asserting `has_errors()` and the ADR failure message.

The new signal orchestrators expose reader-error handling as part of their public contract, but the tests only cover `active_track_id` failure; there is no error-case coverage for `enabled_layers` or `catalogue_bytes` failures. Add tests that exercise those I/O paths, including the per-layer read-error branch that should emit an error finding while continuing other layers.

`calc-adr-user` is described as optional here (“薄い validate に留める（または省略）”), but the same ADR later makes the 8-command taxonomy mandatory in AC-01 (`signal {calc,check}-adr-user,...` must exist). Those two requirements conflict, so the command surface is underspecified.

D7 says chain ⓪ implements only `SoTChain` and that calling `calc` for ⓪ is compile-time forbidden, but D1/AC-01 require `signal calc-adr-user` to exist as a no-persist live calculation command. As written, the trait contract and CLI acceptance criteria cannot both be implemented through the stated trait-driven dispatch; clarify whether `calc-adr-user` is outside `PersistedSoTChain` or add a separate no-persist calc contract.

AC-08 contradicts D7's trait contract. D7 defines three traits and repeatedly states that chain ⓪ `AdrUserChain` must implement `LiveSoTChain` so `signal calc-adr-user` has a typed no-persist `calc_live` path, but the acceptance criteria here require only two traits and say `AdrUserChain` implements `SoTChain` only. If implementers follow AC-08, the planned `calc-adr-user` contract from D1/D7 is dropped, so the plan is internally inconsistent.

The ADR keeps `signal render` open as a future namespace option, but the scope briefing says the user explicitly rejected pushing render into the signal namespace. That leaves a rejected API direction alive instead of recording it as rejected, so downstream planning could implement an option this baseline was supposed to rule out.

D7 relies on `PersistedSoTChain: SoTChain` providing the default `SoTChain::check` flow (`load -> check_freshness -> evaluate_gate`), but a Rust subtrait cannot implement a method declared by its supertrait for all implementers. As written, each persisted chain still has to implement `SoTChain::check` separately, so the plan does not actually force freshness checks through the trait surface and the AC-08 compile-time guarantee is not achievable without changing the trait design (for example, a blanket impl or an explicit helper method pattern).

T007 introduces a new separate public Stage2 entrypoint for `signal check-impl-catalog`, but the infrastructure catalogue still only declares `infrastructure::verify::spec_states::verify_from_spec_json` and explicitly says that function is Stage1-only. As written, the plan and the type catalogue disagree about the exposed public surface, so the Stage2 callable is unmodelled in the plan artifacts.

The error contract for invalid strictness values is inconsistent inside the plan. T003 hard-codes an invalid strictness string to `ParseFailed`, but T013 later treats the same case as `ParseFailed` **or** `InvalidValue`, and the infrastructure catalogue already defines `SignalGatesConfigError::InvalidValue`. That leaves the observable failure mode underspecified and makes the earlier module test target disagree with the later one.

`check_catalogue_spec_signals` is described here as taking `CatalogueSpecSignalsDocument` by value, but the plan/spec require the borrowed form `&CatalogueSpecSignalsDocument` (`plan.md:12`, `spec.md:43`). That leaves the generated contract and the implementation plan disagreeing on the API shape, so downstream work could be generated against the wrong ownership model.

T010 removes the old CLI entrypoints before T011 rewires `Makefile.toml` and CI to the new `signal` commands. That leaves an intermediate state where validation can still invoke deleted commands, so the required between-round `cargo make ci-rust` check can fail until the later task lands. Reorder the Makefile/docs migration ahead of the removals, or merge those steps into one task.

`check_strict_merge_gate` is only specified to resolve `SignalGateMatrix::merge_gate.adr_user`, even though the spec says `signal-gates.json` is the chain × gate strictness source of truth and that the merge gate is wired through all four `signal check-* --gate merge` cells. As written, the plan leaves the existing spec-adr/catalog-spec/impl-catalog merge checks hardcoded strict and ignores their config cells, so the declared matrix cannot actually control all four merge-gate chains.

`SpecAdrChain` claims its persistence target is `<track>/<layer>-catalogue-spec-signals.json`, which is the chain ② per-layer artifact, while the same sentence and the spec define chain ① as using `spec.json`'s `signals` field with a self-consistency check. This contradictory catalogue contract can lead the implementation to read or persist the wrong artifact for `check-spec-adr`.

T010 says the no-alias cleanup is "per CN-06", but CN-06 is the PersistedSoTChain/LiveSoTChain constraint for chain ⓪, not the no-backward-compat requirement. The relevant no-alias requirement is AC-07 / OS-06, so this semantic cross-reference is wrong and breaks the plan traceability for command removal.

The plan places config loading inside `usecase::merge_gate::check_strict_merge_gate` while the loader is declared as `infrastructure::verify::signal_gates_config::load_signal_gates_config`. That forces a usecase-layer function to depend on infrastructure, violating the stated hexagonal layering. Load the file at the CLI/infrastructure boundary or hide it behind a usecase port, then pass a domain `SignalGateMatrix` into the merge-gate logic.

The usecase catalogue says `SpecAdrChain`, `CatalogSpecChain`, and `ImplCatalogChain` obtain `SoTChain` through the `impl<T: PersistedSoTChain> SoTChain for T` blanket impl, but `trait_impls` also declares explicit `domain::SoTChain` impls for those same persisted chains. Following both artifacts would produce conflicting Rust impls; if the entries are only intended as effective impls, the catalogue is semantically misleading. Remove the direct `SoTChain` impl entries for persisted chains or model the blanket impl separately.

`TrackBlobReader::read_adr_verify_report` is declared as requiring a `project_root: PathBuf`, and its docs say it exists to support chain ⓪ evaluation in `check_strict_merge_gate`, but `check_strict_merge_gate` only receives `branch`, `reader`, and `gate_matrix`. The plan therefore has no way for the merge-gate usecase to supply the repo root needed for the ADR directory scan it is required to add, making the chain ⓪ merge-gate task under-specified/infeasible as written.

AC-13 requires unit tests for the new commands, the new trait taxonomy, and config loading, and task coverage marks AC-13 as covered by every task. However the CLI task T009, which is the task that adds all 8 `signal` commands plus aggregate dispatch, contains no test requirement at all; T001 only tests gate resolution helpers, and T013 only covers config loading. As written, the implementation plan can satisfy its listed tasks while still missing the command/dispatch tests explicitly required by AC-13.

`T001` explicitly covers the four trait definitions (`ChainIdentity`, `SoTChain`, `LiveSoTChain`, `PersistedSoTChain`), but the generated table still leaves all four of those entries yellow while only the enums/value objects were promoted to blue. That makes the plan artifacts internally inconsistent about what this round actually landed.

T001 is marked `in_progress`, but its `commit_hash` field was removed instead of remaining explicitly `null`; the same field was also removed from every still-`todo` task. This makes the plan artifact inconsistent with the task-tracking contract that unresolved/in-progress tasks carry a nullable commit slot to be filled when the task is recorded, and it changes T002-T013 even though their semantic state remains `todo`. Restore `"commit_hash": null` for the task entries.

`impl-plan.json` now omits the `commit_hash` field for T002-T013. The plan-artifact task shape and briefing/checklist expect unresolved or in-progress tasks to retain an explicit nullable commit slot (`commit_hash: null`), with only T001 filled after completion. As written, T002 is in progress and T003-T013 are still todo, but their commit slots were removed, making the artifact inconsistent with the task-recording workflow. Restore `"commit_hash": null` for the unresolved task entries.

`task-coverage.json` under-covers AC-14/AC-15: they are only linked to T016/T017, but the Blue transitions still depend on the parser tasks T014/T015 (`QualifiedPath` and `Generic`). As written, the coverage map makes the later catalogue updates look sufficient on their own when they are not.

The blanket-impl where-clause is specified with two incompatible shapes: the ADR/spec prose uses `bound: "domain::PersistedSoTChain"`, while T016 uses `rhs: ["PersistedSoTChain"]`. The plan never reconciles those representations, so the intended JSON shape for `impl_where_predicates` is ambiguous.

`IN-12` / `T015` describe propagating `trait generics + impl_generics` when decoding `methods[].params[].ty` and `methods[].returns`, but the source ADR says the shared context should be `trait generics + method generics` instead. That mismatch leaves method-local generic parameters uncovered and makes the task text inconsistent with its own design reference.

D1 says the catalogue-side `Self::Input<'_>` declaration will be parsed into `Type::QualifiedPath`, and T014 repeats that by asking for a `&Self::Input<'_>` unit test. But the D1 implementation target is specifically `syn::TypePath.qself.is_some()`, which is exercised by fully qualified syntax such as `<Self as ChainIdentity>::Input<'_>`, not plain `Self::Input`. This contradicts the later T016 canonical form and can let T014 pass without testing the new QualifiedPath builder for the actual catalogue form that must become Blue.

`T006` is still marked `in_progress` even though the newly inserted S8 section states that T014-T017 must all complete before T006 resumes. With T014 only in progress and T015-T017 still todo, the plan's active state contradicts its required execution order and can lead the next implementer to continue T006 before the parser/catalogue Blue path is restored.

The execution-order note says the entire S8 group must complete before T006, but the same artifact now marks T006 as done while T015 is still `in_progress`. That leaves the plan self-contradictory about what has to precede T006 and makes the remaining ordering guidance misleading.

D3 is internally inconsistent about the canonical QualifiedPath spelling. The ADR/spec say A-side GAT projections must use `<Self>::Assoc<args>` because `<Self as _>::...` is unparseable, but the same section also says `Some(Path{path:""})` normalizes to `_` and that C-side `<Self as _>::...` and A-side `<Self>::...` converge to the same string. No extra rewrite step is specified, so those claims do not all hold together.

The new associated-item schema is described inconsistently across artifacts. The ADR/spec/plan say `AssocTypeDecl.default` and `AssocConstDecl.default` are `Option<String>`, but `domain-types.json` records `AssocTypeDecl.default` as `Option<TypeRef>` and names the const field `default_value`. That is a concrete contract mismatch between the generated plan artifact and the documented schema.

IN-14 still specifies `AssocTypeDecl.name` and `AssocConstDecl.name` as raw `String` fields, and T017 repeats the same schema, but this same artifact set now catalogs `AssocTypeDecl.name` as `TypeName` and `AssocConstDecl.name` as `AssocConstName` in `domain-types.json`. That is a semantic contradiction in the plan artifacts: the spec/plan tell implementers to use primitive strings while the rendered domain catalogue records validated identifier newtypes for the implemented design. Align IN-14/T017/ADR D4 with the validated newtype shape so the plan has one authoritative schema.

D1 still describes `<Self as ChainIdentity>::Input<'_>` as the catalogue-side canonical qualified path that will match rustdoc, but D3 later says the catalogue canonical form must be `<Self>::Assoc` because rustdoc Self projections arrive with an empty trait path and explicit `<Self as Trait>::Assoc` will not compare correctly. These are mutually exclusive conventions for the same GAT projection declarations, so the ADR leaves readers with two incompatible implementation rules. Update the D1 text to mark the explicit-trait form as the pre-D3 assumption or align it with the D3 `<Self>::...` convention.

ADR context still says rustdoc/formatter render `Self::Input` as `<Self as ChainIdentity>::Input`, but the later D3 root-cause section says rustdoc emits an empty trait path and the pre-D3 C-side string was `<Self as >::Input`, with the accepted canonical catalogue path being `<Self>::...` rendered as `<Self as _>::...`. These are conflicting descriptions of the same GAT projection behavior, so the ADR leaves readers with two incompatible formatter contracts.

`AC-18` is mapped only to `T017`, but `AC-18` requires the actual `domain-types.json` assoc item declarations and a Blue `calc-impl-catalog` result. `T017` only adds the schema/codecs; the plan puts the catalogue declarations and signal verification in `T018`/later. As written, task coverage claims AC-18 is satisfied by a task that cannot complete the criterion, leaving the acceptance dependency order inconsistent.

The S8 narrative still says T016-T019 are implemented in the worktree but uncommitted and instructs the next operator to commit them in a future order, while the task entries in the same artifact now mark T016-T019 as done with commit hash f3e3b9b5. That leaves the plan asserting both that the work remains uncommitted and that it has already been committed, which can misdirect the next implementation/review step.

T008 still states that there are "no public API surface changes," but the same plan-artifacts change adds two `infrastructure::verify::catalogue_spec_signals::*` functions as `action: "add"` FreeFunction entries whose docs say they are exposed for integration tests. That is a semantic contradiction in the plan: reviewers and implementers cannot tell whether T008 is expected to preserve the public API or intentionally add these public helpers. Update the T008 description or the catalogue entries so the public API intent is consistent.

`compute_catalogue_declaration_hash` is documented as part of chain ② `catalogue-spec-signals` stale detection and says callers compare a stored `declaration_hash`, but the same plan defines chain ② (`CatalogSpecChain` / `check_catalog_spec`) as using `entry_hash` freshness while `declaration_hash` belongs to chain ③ (`ImplCatalogChain` / `check_impl_catalog`). That leaves the plan with two conflicting stale-detection contracts for chain ②, so an implementer or test author could validate the catalogue-spec gate against the wrong hash granularity.

T020 directs the new usecase orchestrators to compute the per-layer SHA-256 themselves and says that SHA-256 computation is confined to those usecase functions, but ADR D8-4 assigns catalog SHA-256 calculation to `libs/domain/src/` as an invariant primitive. That leaves the plan internally inconsistent: implementing T020 as written would violate the ADR/AC-21 placement table it claims to satisfy. Align the task/usecase docs with D8-4, or relax D8-4 to allow the usecase layer to own hashing.

T020/S9 place the new argless orchestrators, including the per-layer loop and active-track resolution, under `libs/usecase/src/signal/`, and the type catalogue declares them as `usecase::signal::*`. That contradicts the ADR added in the same amendment, whose D8-4 placement table requires per-layer iteration and active-track resolution to live in `libs/usecase/src/chain/`. Following the plan as written would either violate the ADR or produce implementations in a module different from the catalogue, so the plan/ADR/catalogue need one consistent placement before implementation.

T020 makes the new usecase orchestrators responsible for active-branch resolution, reading layer configuration, path construction, SHA-256 calculation, and direct infrastructure primitive dispatch while their planned signatures only accept `workspace_root`/`strict`. That contradicts the cited hexagonal convention (`usecase -> domain` only; external I/O and infrastructure access must be behind ports/adapters) and also makes the planned mocked usecase tests infeasible because there is no port or adapter parameter to mock.

D8-5 classifies `execute_verify_adr_signals_with_strict` as one of the `per-layer primitive`s called from the usecase loop. ADR-user verification is chain ⓪/repo-global, not a TDDD per-layer catalogue primitive, and the calc primitives needed by T020 are not named here. This makes the D8 boundary guidance semantically wrong for implementers wiring the per-layer refactor.

T020/D8 asks `libs/usecase` orchestrators to read working-tree files and directly call `libs/infrastructure` calc/check primitives. That contradicts the stated hexagonal boundary: usecase may orchestrate domain/usecase logic, while infrastructure access must be behind adapters/ports built by `apps/cli-composition`. With the proposed signatures taking only `workspace_root`, implementing this plan either violates layer dependencies/usecase purity or fails AC-21’s placement requirement.

T020's rendered task text still specifies the usecase functions as taking only `workspace_root`/`strict` and says each orchestrator calls the matching infrastructure primitive in its loop. That contradicts ADR D8-5 and `impl-plan.json`, which require infrastructure primitives to be injected via `per_layer_fn` because the usecase layer cannot import infrastructure. An implementer following this rendered plan would violate the intended architecture boundary.

The rendered usecase catalogue lists the new `usecase::signal::*` functions without the required `per_layer_fn: F` dependency-injection parameter, while `usecase-types.json` declares that parameter and ADR D8-5 requires it. This leaves the human-readable type view pointing at signatures that cannot satisfy the approved D8 wiring model.

T020 assigns `result rendering` to `apps/cli-composition/src/signal.rs`, but ADR D8-1/D8-4 put `CommandOutcome` rendering exclusively in `apps/cli/src/commands/signal/`, and D8-2 limits cli-composition to adapter graph construction, infrastructure closure wiring, and the usecase call. As written, the plan gives implementers conflicting layer placement instructions and can violate CN-17/AC-21.

The check orchestrator catalogue types require `per_layer_fn` to accept a fourth `bool` argument, but ADR D8-5 specifies the injected TDDD calc/check primitive shape as `Fn(LayerId, &Path, &str) -> VerifyOutcome`. Implementing the type catalogue will not satisfy the ADR signature, while implementing the ADR leaves these check entries wrong; align D8-5 or the two check function entries.

T020 makes `workspace_root` the boundary for filesystem access and instructs the new usecase orchestrators to read the active-track id/layer config and hash `<layer>-types.json` directly from the working tree. That conflicts with the referenced hexagonal convention/usecase purity rule: usecase may orchestrate domain logic and injected ports/functions, but file/worktree I/O belongs behind an adapter or port. Implementing this literally would either violate the architecture checks or require redesigning the task during implementation.

CN-16 says `signal check --gate commit|merge` must be callable “argless”, and AC-20 repeats that phrasing, but the command shown literally requires `--gate` while the existing gate design also depends on `check-* --gate commit|merge` to resolve strictness. This makes the planned CLI contract contradictory: implementers cannot tell whether the requirement is truly zero arguments or only “no per-layer flags”.

`SignalLayerReader` exposes `catalogue_path(layer)` and its docs say the cli-composition adapter constructs the path, but CN-17/AC-21/D8-4 assign per-layer signals/catalogue path construction to domain/usecase and keep cli-composition wiring-only. As written, the type catalogue reintroduces the adapter-side path construction the new constraints are trying to prohibit.

T020 still tells implementers that the cli-composition closure captures path context and even the layer path from the adapter filesystem context, while CN-17/AC-21 assign per-layer signals/catalogue path construction to domain/usecase and limit cli-composition to wiring. This leaves the round-9 architecture issue unresolved: the plan can still be implemented by constructing paths in cli-composition instead of the domain/usecase boundary required by the spec.

D8-2 still shows the injected closure as `|layer_id, path, hash| ...`, which contradicts D8-5 and the type catalogue's `per_layer_fn: Fn(LayerId, &str) -> VerifyOutcome` contract. Because this ADR is the source for T020, the remaining three-argument example preserves the old path-threading design and makes the intended function signature inconsistent across the plan artifacts.

AC-21 still requires the path-construction logic to live in `libs/domain/` or `libs/usecase/`, while the new D8 design explicitly assigns signals-file path construction to infrastructure primitives. Because T020 and the usecase type docs follow the D8 model (`per_layer_fn(layer_id, hash)` and infra constructs the output path), this acceptance criterion would reject the intended implementation or push path construction back into the wrong layer. Update AC-21 to exclude path construction from the domain/usecase-only list or explicitly allow `libs/infrastructure/` for signals-file output paths.

CN-17 still permits per-layer `signals / catalogue` path construction in `domain / usecase or infrastructure`, while D8-4 and AC-21 assign signals-file output path construction specifically to `libs/infrastructure/src/`. This leaves T020 implementers able to put signals path construction in domain/usecase and still satisfy CN-17, contradicting the ADR/AC boundary that this round is meant to make strict.

The `SignalLayerReader` contract does not make the stated usecase-owned active-track resolution executable: `active_track_id()` is a reader method, but `catalogue_bytes(layer)` has no `TrackId` parameter for the usecase to pass the resolved ID back. As specified, the adapter must either resolve/cache the active track internally or ignore `active_track_id`, contradicting D8-4/AC-21's placement of active-track resolution in domain/usecase.

T020/type catalogue gives the check orchestrators a `strict: bool` parameter while their injected `per_layer_fn` is still `Fn(LayerId, &str) -> VerifyOutcome`, and D8-5 says the check closure captures `strict` in cli-composition so the usecase closure signature carries no bool. With the current signature, the usecase has no defined way to apply or pass that `strict` value, and the generated docs even claim it is “passed directly” to a callable that does not accept it. This leaves implementers with two conflicting designs for strictness ownership in `check_impl_catalog` / `check_catalog_spec`; either remove `strict` from the usecase functions and capture it in the closure, or change the closure contract consistently.

D8 still embeds the implementation task id `T020` in the ADR text, despite the plan-artifacts briefing requiring D8 to have no track-specific T## references. This makes the long-lived design decision depend on a transient impl-plan task; move the task-specific wording to the impl plan and keep the ADR phrased in stable design terms.

`SignalLayerReaderError::Io` is declared as a unit variant, but the docs say it wraps filesystem / OS errors. As written, implementers cannot preserve or propagate the underlying IO error with `?` without discarding it; either give `Io` a source payload or change the contract to explicitly describe lossy mapping.

S9 says active-track resolution, layer enumeration, and hash computation are confined to the usecase layer, but ADR D8-4 assigns TDDD layer enumeration to a domain primitive. That leaves T020 with conflicting layer-boundary instructions for where the layer set is owned; align the plan with D8-4 or relax D8-4 explicitly.

`SignalLayerReader` is documented as implemented in `apps/cli-composition/src/signal.rs`, but that trait owns `active_track_id`, `enabled_layers`, and `catalogue_bytes`, which are the active-track/layer/filesystem responsibilities that CN-17 and ADR D8-2/D8-4 explicitly keep out of cli-composition. This makes the plan point the adapter implementation at a forbidden layer instead of an infrastructure adapter that cli-composition merely wires in.

T020 describes the strictness closure for “check variants” as passing strict to `infra_check_impl_catalog(layer_id, hash, strict)`. That primitive only matches chain ③; `check_catalog_spec` needs the corresponding catalog-spec check primitive called out by ADR D8-5 and the usecase catalogue. As written, the plan either wires chain ② to the impl-catalog checker or leaves that checker unspecified, contradicting AC-20’s requirement that both check commands work correctly.

CN-17 allows active-track resolution and TDDD layer enumeration to be placed in either `domain` or `usecase`, but the cited ADR D8-4 placement table is more specific: active-track resolution belongs in `libs/usecase/src/`, while TDDD layer enumeration belongs in `libs/domain/src/`. This makes the contract satisfiable by placements the ADR explicitly disallows, so T020 could pass the new CN/AC while still violating the architecture decision.

T020/plan.md now marks the argless CLI refactor as done and states that CLI commands drop `--signals-path`/`--catalog-hash`, cli-composition only wires `Fn(LayerId, &str)`, and infrastructure primitives construct the signals-file path from the layer ID. The infrastructure catalogue still declares `check_catalog_spec_from_signals_file` and `check_impl_catalog_from_signals_file` as taking `signals_path: &Path` and describes them as called by `signal check-* --signals-path P --catalog-hash H --gate ...`. That is an internal plan-artifact contradiction: the recorded T020 design cannot be wired without reintroducing path construction or the removed CLI flags.

T020 says cli-composition must wire layer-id/hash closures and must not construct paths, but the infrastructure catalogue still exposes the chain ②/③ check boundary as explicit `signals_path` APIs and does not declare the required layer-id wrapper/equivalent. As written, the plan artifacts leave no catalogued infrastructure entrypoint that satisfies the D8 placement rule, so implementation must either put path construction back in cli-composition or rely on an undeclared helper.

T020 is marked done in the plan, but the infrastructure catalogue still renders required T020 items as Signal 🟡: `LocalSignalLayerReaderAdapter` plus the two `*_for_layer` primitives. The task text says these pieces are implemented by T020, so the plan artifacts simultaneously claim completion and show the required infrastructure contract as not Blue. Keep T020 open or update/regenerate the infrastructure signals so the completed plan state is coherent.

`LocalSignalLayerReaderAdapter` is specified as resolving the active track and enumerating TDDD-enabled layers from `architecture-rules.json`, but the D8/CN-17 boundary assigns active-track resolution to the usecase orchestrator and layer enumeration to a domain primitive. As written, the plan artifact places core orchestration/domain logic in an infrastructure adapter, so T020 would implement the responsibility split in the wrong layer.

`check_catalog_spec_for_layer` is documented as constructing the track-scoped signals path, but its signature only receives `layer_id`, `catalog_hash_hex`, and `strict`. With no `TrackId` or workspace/root context, the only way to locate `track/items/<track-id>/<layer>-catalogue-spec-signals.json` is to rediscover the active track inside infrastructure, which contradicts CN-17/D8's placement of active-track resolution in the usecase layer. The same issue applies to `check_impl_catalog_for_layer`.

The contract map reuses the same Mermaid node IDs for distinct functions, for example `F50_usecase_usecase_usecase__signal__calc_catalog_spec` and `F50_usecase_usecase_usecase__signal__calc_impl_catalog` share the `F50...` ID, and the same pattern appears for the check functions and new infrastructure wrappers. Mermaid node IDs define identity, so these entries collapse into the same graph nodes and the map no longer represents the separate contracts accurately.

The rendered usecase catalogue contradicts the planned T020 API shape: the four `usecase::signal::*` rows are shown as existing Blue functions taking `reader: &R, per_layer_fn: F`, while the plan requires new argless CLI-facing orchestrators taking `workspace_root: PathBuf` and remaining Yellow until implementation. As written, the plan artifact no longer describes the future work T020 is supposed to implement.

T020 makes active-track resolution an internal usecase-owned step, but the declared orchestrator callback only receives `(LayerId, &str)`. The same plan/catalogue also introduces infrastructure per-layer check primitives that require the usecase-resolved `TrackId`; with this signature, cli-composition cannot supply that TrackId without resolving it itself, contradicting the stated D8 ownership rule and making the described wiring infeasible. The plan should pass the resolved TrackId through the callback or otherwise define how the infrastructure wrapper receives it.

T020 now specifies `per_layer_fn: Fn(TrackId, LayerId, &str)` and says infrastructure path wrappers receive the usecase-resolved `TrackId`, but the cited ADR D8 contract says cli-composition injects `per_layer_fn: Fn(LayerId, &str) -> VerifyOutcome` and that the closure signature must not include path/track context. This plan therefore no longer conforms to the D8 dependency contract it claims to implement; an implementer following it would build a different usecase/infrastructure boundary than the ADR authorizes.

T020 requires all four usecase orchestrators to be wired through per-layer infrastructure closures of shape `|layer_id, hash| ...`, including the two calc paths. The infrastructure catalogue only adds per-layer wrappers for the check paths and still exposes the calc/catalog-spec entrypoints as the older whole-command functions with `items_dir`, `track_id`, `workspace_root`, and `strict`; there is no corresponding per-layer calc primitive for `calc_catalog_spec` or `calc_impl_catalog`. As written, the plan artifacts do not provide an executable infrastructure contract for the two argless calc orchestrators T020 says are done.

T020 makes the usecase resolve the active TrackId, then calls the injected `per_layer_fn` as `Fn(LayerId, &str)` while also saying cli-composition must not resolve active tracks. The new infrastructure wrappers, however, require `track_id: domain::TrackId` to construct `track/items/<track-id>/...` paths. With the TrackId neither passed to the closure nor allowed to be resolved in cli-composition, the described wiring is not executable without violating the responsibility split; pass the resolved TrackId through the injected callback or change the wrapper boundary consistently.

T020 claims ADR D8 conformance while changing the injected primitive signature to `Fn(&TrackId, LayerId, &str)` and requiring the closure to receive the usecase-resolved track ID. ADR D8-2/D8-5 fixes that boundary as `Fn(LayerId, &str) -> VerifyOutcome`, with cli-composition only wiring a layer/hash closure and the infrastructure primitive deriving its own path context. As written, the plan artifacts and ADR assert incompatible dependency-injection contracts, so implementers cannot tell which boundary is authoritative.

T020 makes active-track resolution explicitly usecase-owned, but the new per-layer infrastructure primitives no longer accept the resolved `TrackId` while still claiming they derive `track/items/<track-id>/...` paths from only `workspace_root` and `layer_id`. That leaves no way for the infrastructure call to use the track selected by the usecase without re-resolving active track inside infrastructure, which contradicts the stated D8 boundary and can check/write a different track if active state changes between calls. Pass the resolved track identity through the injected closure/infrastructure primitive, or revise the responsibility split so the plan is executable without violating D8.

T020 now states that cli-composition resolves the active TrackId with the same SignalLayerReader adapter and supplies that resolved track context to the infrastructure primitive, but the infrastructure catalogue for the new per-layer primitives says cli-composition does not resolve the active track and the listed signatures only accept workspace_root/layer_id/hash. That leaves the plan with two incompatible ownership models for track resolution and path derivation, so implementers cannot satisfy both the no-race requirement in impl-plan.json and the infrastructure type contract as written.

T020 contradicts the stated D8/CN-17 responsibility split: it first says active-track resolution is a usecase-owned step, but then requires `apps/cli-composition` to resolve the active `TrackId` separately and capture it for the infrastructure closure. D8 says cli-composition only wires the `SignalLayerReader` adapter and passes `per_layer_fn: Fn(LayerId, &str)`, so this plan leaks active-track resolution into the composition layer and makes the design inconsistent.

The new per-layer infrastructure wrappers are specified with only `workspace_root`, `layer_id`, and hash/strict parameters, but their docs require them to derive `track/items/<track-id>/...` paths while the plan also says active-track resolution is confined to the usecase and cli-composition does not resolve the active track. With no `TrackId` in the wrapper contract, the infrastructure layer must either rediscover the active track itself, violating the stated D8 ownership, or cannot construct the track-scoped paths. This makes the T020 plan internally inconsistent for all four `*_for_layer` primitives.
